operational.body: Morning. The floor was a wreck at close — boxes in the aisles, two unfinished price stickers on used carts. Fix the basics or we have a longer conversation.
I left the open list on the register. Get it done before we unlock. Twelve years this store has been here; I'm not watching it slide on a clipboard.

sales.body: Sales were thin yesterday. Either you reset the floor today or I rewrite the schedule. Your call.
Numbers are not moving. Reprice the used wall, run someone at the door, do something visible. I'm watching.

staff.body: Heard you ran someone off again. We don't have benches deep enough for that. Whoever's left covers the gap — make sure they actually do.
Two complaints from the back. I don't care who's right. You're the one on the floor. Sort it before open.

operational.body: Yesterday was fine. Not great, not bad. Pull from the back early, sweep the entry mat, and we'll see how today reads.
I logged the safe at 6:40. Float looked right. Keep the open list on the wall — saves us both a conversation later.

sales.body: Decent ring count yesterday. The used cart near the door is doing work — keep it stocked all day.
Light on sales. Check the pricing on the older inventory. Sometimes that's all it is.

staff.body: Coverage looked thin during the lunch hour. Talk to whoever's on next so we don't run that gap again.
Payroll cleared. People notice when checks land on time — keep doing that.

operational.body: Floor looked sharp at close. I appreciate that you're staying on top of the resets. Keep it loose today — Tuesday's usually slow.
Receiving dock said our order's bumped to before noon. That should buy you a clean afternoon. Use it.

sales.body: Strong day yesterday. The retro cart by the entrance is paying off. If you need a 10% markdown on the older stock, I'll authorize it.
Slow Tuesday. Happens. Try a bundle on the stuff that's been sitting — your call on what.

staff.body: Crew read good yesterday. Whoever closed left the back tidy — pass that along. Small things matter.
If we lose anyone this week I'll put two new candidates in the pool. Don't burn yourself out trying to cover both shifts.

operational.body: I'm at the area meeting all morning. You've got the floor. Open list is the same as Monday's — handle it your way.
Don't bother with the open checklist today. You know the floor better than the form does. Just get us to noon.

sales.body: Best week in months. I told the area director about the front-cart approach. They want to talk. Keep going.
Slow day yesterday — happens to every store. I trust your read on what to push today. Don't second-guess yourself.

staff.body: Crew likes working your shifts. I notice that. If you need a hand from the pool for the weekend, say the word.
Three weeks straight, payroll clean, no walkouts. That's real management. I've flagged you for the next review cycle.

day_1.body:
First day. RetroVault's been here twelve years and I plan to keep it that way. Punch in at the back, walk the aisles, get a feel for the floor. The clipboard at the register has the open list. I'll check in around lunch.

Don't try to be heroic — just keep customers moving and the shelves honest. We'll talk specifics at the end of the day.

— Vic

day_10.body:
Ten days in. The mall office sent over the quarterly sales report — we're middle of the pack, which is better than this store has read in a year. Don't get comfortable. The next stretch is where most fill-ins crack. Just keep doing what's working.

— Vic

day_20.body:
Twenty down, ten to go. Area director swung by the dock last night and asked about you by name — that doesn't happen often. Whatever you're doing on the floor, keep doing it. The last week is where we get evaluated, so don't slack now.

— Vic

trade_in_intake.body:
Mall office cleared us for trade-in intake. There's a new station at the counter — used carts, used handhelds, whatever the customer drags in. Quote fair, log it, hold it 24 hours before it hits the floor.

Don't overpay. The margin lives in the back room.

— Vic

tournament_events.body:
We're cleared to run weekend tournaments. The schedule rack is on the side of the back office — fill in the slots and the front kiosk will take signups. Tournaments are loss leaders, so don't price the entry like a sale; the win is the foot traffic.

— Vic

pricing_authority.body:
You've got pricing authority on used inventory now. That means real authority — if you bury a sticker, it's on you, not me. Use the resolver, watch the trend board, and don't run promotions past close without flagging me.

— Vic

supplier_tier_2.body:
Distributor moved us up a tier. New SKUs in the catalog this morning — better margins, but minimum-order rules apply. Read the order screen carefully before you commit. We don't eat dead stock cleanly.

— Vic

employee_tradein_certified.body:
By the way — trade-in terminal is yours now. Don't undervalue anything.

— Vic

employee_holdlist_access.body:
Hold list terminal is open to you. Don't move anyone's reservation without asking me first.

— Vic

employee_display_authority.body:
You can set the featured display. Use it.

— Vic

employee_closing_certified.body:
You're on close tonight. Checklist is behind the register.

— Vic

zero_sales.body: I checked the register tape. Zero. Not one transaction. Whatever you were doing back there, it wasn't selling.
You ran a store today. Zero sales. I'd say something, but I'm not sure it'd land.
Empty register at close. The mall doesn't run on ambiance.

empty_shelves.body: Customers came in and left with nothing because you had nothing to sell them. That's the whole problem in one sentence.
Empty shelves at close. People don't come back to a store that can't be bothered to stock itself.
You turned customers away today. With empty shelves. The backroom situation needs to be your first problem tomorrow.

stockout_walkouts.body: Multiple customers left because you didn't have what they wanted. That's not bad luck — that's a stocking problem.
People walked out today because there was nothing on the shelf for them. They don't give second chances, usually.
Walkouts on record. Three separate customers came in, found nothing, and left. Figure out what they came for.

queue_timeout.body: Customers waited, gave up, and left. That's the checkout line making decisions for you.
Four-plus queue timeouts today. Customers aren't paid to wait. Your line is losing you business.
Wait times pushed people out the door. Whatever is slowing down checkout needs to be looked at seriously.

normal.body: Numbers are what they are. Be here tomorrow.
Day's done. Margin is thin. Do better with the floor tomorrow.
Closed out. Nothing remarkable. That's either consistent or stagnant — depends which way you're moving.

zero_sales.body: Tough day — nothing moved. Worth taking a look at what you were priced at and whether anyone could actually find what they needed.
No sales today. Could be foot traffic, could be inventory, could be a dozen other things. Think about what was different.
Zero on the board. It happens. Less so when things are stocked and prices are reasonable, but it happens.

empty_shelves.body: You ran dry and it cost you sales. Restock protocols need attention — backroom items should be hitting shelves before customers arrive, not after they leave.
Sold out is a nice problem to have once in a while. Every day is not once in a while. Look at your reorder timing.
Clean shelves look great in a photo. In practice it means you ran out of things to sell. Get ahead of that.

stockout_walkouts.body: A few customers walked today over stock gaps. That's recoverable if you treat it as a signal about what to carry.
Walkouts this shift — not a crisis, but worth noting the pattern. What were the gaps and can you fill them?
Some traffic came in and left empty-handed. Better availability tomorrow would convert at least some of those.

queue_timeout.body: Checkout backed up enough today that people bailed before they got to the register. That's a flow problem worth solving before it becomes a reputation problem.
Queue timeouts this shift. If it's staffing, fix staffing. If it's layout, fix layout. People won't wait indefinitely.
A few customers timed out in the queue today. Not a catastrophe, but it signals something is bottlenecking at close of the sale loop.

normal.body: Day is logged. Keep the floor moving and the stock visible.
Close is done. Standard day — nothing that breaks the run in either direction.
Wrapped up. Inventory and price points are the two things most in your control. Worth reviewing.

zero_sales.body: Rough one today — nothing rang up. You've had better days. Figure out what the bottleneck was and you'll bounce back.
Zero sales. I know you've been putting in the work, so something specific went sideways — worth tracking down.
Not your best day, but the pattern has been solid. One goose egg doesn't define a run.

empty_shelves.body: Ran out of shelf stock today — I can see you're moving product, which is the good news. The bad news is people walked who might have bought. Tighten up the restock window.
High sell-through, zero backfill — the backroom–shelf pipeline needs work. You're leaving sales on the table.
Burned through everything and had nothing to show at close. That's a hustle problem, not a demand problem. Fix the flow.

stockout_walkouts.body: A handful of walkouts today — I know you're managing a lot of moving pieces, but availability is the floor. What slipped through?
Some people left without buying because the thing they wanted wasn't there. You've been running a tight ship otherwise — what happened on the stock side today?
Walkouts flagged in the system. If it's a one-off, fine. If it's a pattern, it's worth building a checklist around.

queue_timeout.body: Longer waits than ideal today — I can see volume was up, which explains part of it. But the queue abandonment is worth watching as you scale.
Some checkout friction today — people getting stuck in the line. You've handled higher volume before. What was different about the flow?
Queue timeouts flagged. High demand is a good problem, but not if we lose the sale anyway at the register. Think about throughput at peak hours.

normal.body: Good close. The trend line is holding — keep doing what's working.
Solid day. Appreciate the consistency you're bringing to the floor.
End of day looks reasonable. You know what to tune — trust your read on it.

zero_sales.body: Dead day. Genuinely surprised given where things have been — something unusual happened. Talk me through it when you get a chance.
Zero moves me to want to understand, not judge. What was the floor like? What were you stocked with?
Not your day. But after everything we've built here, I'm treating this as an outlier until I see otherwise.

empty_shelves.body: You had demand and no supply to meet it. That's a painful gap to close after the fact. The sell-through rate looks great — just need the refill cycle to keep up with it.
Shelves empty, walkouts on the clock. The sales engine is running, but the inventory side isn't keeping pace. This is a solvable problem and you know this store.
High velocity, low supply. The good version of a hard day. Demand is clearly there — now make sure product is too.

stockout_walkouts.body: Caught some walkouts in the log today — unusual for you. Something must have hit the supply chain differently this shift. I'm not worried, but I wanted to flag it.
A few left without buying. Given your track record I'm guessing it wasn't for lack of trying. What's the read?
Walkouts on an otherwise solid shift. Probably a gap in a specific category — worth checking what got missed.

queue_timeout.body: Busy day turned into wait-time losses — you had the customers, just not quite the throughput to cash them out. That's a capacity question, not a hustle question.
The queue backed up past the patience threshold today. With volume like this, it might be time to think about checkout staffing at peak. You'd know better than I would.
Timeouts at the register today. Given how consistently you've run this floor, I'm guessing it was a volume spike rather than a process breakdown. Worth a postmortem.

normal.body: Nice work today. This place runs differently when someone's paying attention, and it shows.
Strong close. You've earned the trust of this floor — it's showing in the numbers.
Day's done and it shows in the right way. We're building something here.

fallback.body:
Walk the floor, run the register, keep the shelves honest. I'll be in the back if you need me.

— Vic
